summary:
After severing the cranial portion of the accessory nerve, only the stylopharyngeus muscle remains functionally unaffected due to its dependence primarily on cranial nerve IX for movement during swallowing. This is critical as other pharyngeal structures may be compromised without proper neuromodulation through both glossopharyngeal and vagus pathways. \boxed{C}

The summarized version captures the essence while adhering strictly to two concise statements focused directly on clinically pertinent information related to the patient’s condition regarding preserved muscular activity despite neurosurgical intervention affecting one particular cranial nerve component. Agent1 reasoned that among various swallow-related musculature, the stylopharynx uniquely depends on an alternate pathway via Cranial Nerve IX when facing disruption along with the Accessory Nerve's craniocervical segment; hence maintaining operational integrity under such circumstances.
